section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
paragraphs.text: 7 October 2025

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
paragraphs.text: ITI - the Information Technology Industry Council - is the global trade association of the technology industry, representing 80 of the world's most innovative tech companies. Our membership spans across the entire spectrum of technology, including global leaders in software and AI, hardware, cloud, cybersecurity and semiconductors.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Digital services play a foundational role in driving innovation and economic growth, enabling new business models and creating opportunities for businesses of all sizes to reach users, markets, and consumers across the EU. As the representative body for the global tech industry, ITI supports efforts by the European Commission to uphold consumer rights and ensure that digital services are safe, fair, and trustworthy. We recognize the shared responsibility across industry, regulators, and society to create and maintain an inclusive, secure, and innovation-conducive digital environment. Our members are committed to ensuring that their services meet high standards of safety and fairness, recognizing that maintaining user trust is also essential for long-term business viability and competitiveness.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: ITI appreciates the opportunity to respond to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the proposed Digital Fairness Act (DFA). The Digital Fairness Fitness Check found that the existing EU consumer protection acquis has only partially met its goals in the digital context, pointing to fragmented and inconsistent enforcement. In our view, these shortcomings stem primarily from oversight challenges rather than substantive gaps in the law. The Fitness Check also rightly notes that the impact of recently adopted legislation, including the Digital Markets Act (DMA), Digital Services Act (DSA), Data Act, and the Artificial Intelligence Act, cannot yet be fully assessed, as these instruments are still in the early stages of implementation. These frameworks have a wide range of provisions that directly address many of the issues raised in the DFA Call for Evidence. In addition, the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), Unfair Contract Terms Directive (UCTD), and GDPR already address several of the practices raised in the consultation, such as manipulative design, deceptive practices, and unfair contract terms. The EU consumer protection framework was most recently updated in 2019 as part of the Modernisation Directive (2019/2161) and is principles-based, allowing it to adapt to new market dynamics and technological developments. The European Commission published its first evaluation of the Modernisation Directive in June 2024, noting that many Member States had missed the transposition deadline, and that the Directive was in a “relatively early stage of application”.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: In light of this, while we share the overarching objective of reviewing and streamlining consumer protection in the digital economy, we do not believe that introducing new legislative measures at this stage would be the most effective course of action. This is

I. Key Messages and Recommendations

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: info@itic.org www.itic.org @iti_techtweets

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: because the effect of the most recent consumer framework revision cannot be assessed and landmark legislation with important contributions to consumers' experiences, rights, and safety online is still very new. Instead, priority should be given to the effective implementation of recently adopted rules, particularly the DSA, DMA, and AI Act, through measures that strengthen oversight and promote regulatory consistency across Member States.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Before introducing a new legislative initiative, a comprehensive mapping exercise of existing legislation would help identify whether there are any specific and persistent gaps in specific consumer protection laws that may require further attention. Any new legislative intervention, should it be deemed necessary, should be highly targeted, evidence-based, and carefully designed to fill identifiable gaps while avoiding overlaps with existing legislation. The introduction of additional obligations without a clear, evidence-based justification such as a demonstrated market failure or widespread consumer harm, risks increasing legal fragmentation, overlapping requirements, and increased complexity and uncertainty. This could ultimately make it harder for businesses to comply and for authorities to enforce, while also raising the risk of higher consumer prices.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: We therefore encourage the Commission to uphold the principles of Better Regulation, which require that all new initiatives be necessary, proportionate, and supported by clear evidence of added value. In line with the Commission's broader goals around competitiveness and simplification, any future proposals should meet a high threshold of necessity and demonstrate tangible policy benefits.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: This response sets out ITI's key priorities in detail; the main recommendations are summarized below:

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Rigorously apply Better Regulation principles, including the requirement to demonstrate market failure or consumer harm and evaluate proportionality and necessity before pursuing new rules.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Conduct a comprehensive mapping of existing legislation relevant to the issues raised in the Call for Evidence to determine whether they are already sufficiently addressed in law, ensuring that any new intervention avoids overlaps and focuses on genuine regulatory gaps.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Phase next steps to allow sufficient time to evaluate the real-world impact of the Modernisation Directive, DMA, DSA, Data Act, and AI Act before considering additional measures.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Provisions related to consent and personalization must remain fully aligned with the logic of the GDPR and the ePrivacy regime to avoid regulatory fragmentation.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Focus on implementation and streamlining oversight by enhancing coordination across authorities and supporting the development of joint guidance to guide company compliance.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Safeguard legal certainty by upholding established consumer law concepts and ensuring balanced consumer protection in the digital age.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
I. Key Messages and Recommendations
paragraphs.text: Ensure that any potential DFA initiative contributes to the EU's broader simplification agenda by being developed holistically alongside the Digital Omnibus package, with the aim of streamlining the existing digital rulebook.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Demonstrating necessity before legislating
paragraphs.text: In assessing the need for any future Digital Fairness Act initiative, it is crucial to adhere closely to Better Regulation principles. Before proposing any new legislative proposal, the Commission must demonstrate that:

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Demonstrating necessity before legislating
paragraphs.text: There are clear and specific market failures and identifiable gaps that are not already addressed under existing or recently adopted legislation, and these result in real, evidenced and concrete consumer harm.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Demonstrating necessity before legislating
paragraphs.text: A rigorous and evidence-based impact assessment is conducted to evaluate whether the objectives of the DFA can be met through streamlining implementation and more efficient oversight rather than new rules.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Demonstrating necessity before legislating
paragraphs.text: Given the current mandate's focus on competitiveness, it is crucial to also evaluate whether any future legislative initiative is both fostering competitiveness and supporting innovation while also strengthening consumer protection.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Demonstrating necessity before legislating
paragraphs.text: Any new measures are proportionate, coherent and do not overlap or conflict with the broader EU digital framework.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: In addition to demonstrating necessity, it is essential to carefully consider timing. The impact assessment should build on the Fitness Check analysis and carefully account for the recent adoption of the DMA, DSA, and AI Act as well as evaluate the impact of the Modernisation Directive. The CRD and UCPD were also both recently revised. In May 2022, the European Commission proposed to amend the CRD (Directive 2011/83/EU) with a Directive concerning financial services contracts concluded at a distance (Directive 2023/2673), notably with provisions about withdrawal functions from online contracts. In March 2022, the Commission proposed amendments to the UCPD (Directive 2005/29/EC) and the CRD with the Directive empowering consumers for the green transition (2024/825), which banned deceptive practices like greenwashing.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: These frameworks are still in the early phases of implementation, and their effectiveness has yet to be fully evaluated. Adequate time must be allowed to assess whether they can address the identified challenges, as meaningful evaluation will only be feasible once sufficient experience with their application has been gained. The Commission should therefore consider forbearance in these areas, and recommend a further review once implementation is complete and an appropriate period of time has passed to allow for regulators and organizations to adapt.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: Strengthening the consultation process to support evidence-based outcomes

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: To make the impact assessment process more robust, the consultation questionnaire itself could be improved to better facilitate evidence-based policymaking. Its current design may limit the ability of respondents to provide full and nuanced input, may not capture the full range of stakeholder views and risks skewing the overall picture of stakeholder preferences.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: For example, the structure of the questionnaire relies on conditional measurement: respondents who indicate that no new action is needed are not given the opportunity to explain their reasoning, while those selecting other options can elaborate. In addition, survey logic continues to direct participants to questions on new actions even when they have explicitly selected alternatives such as better enforcement or non-regulatory measures. The structure of closed-ended questions may influence respondents' responses to later open-ended questions by priming them to consider specific types of interventions that might not have otherwise been top-of-mind. In addition, key terms such as "dark patterns", "unfair personalization", and "addictive design" are not sufficiently defined, which can lead to divergent interpretations among respondents. Finally, the questionnaire does not allow respondents to rank, express trade-offs between issues, or directly adjudicate the intensity of their preferences. Restructuring the consultation to address these points would enable more balanced feedback and provide stronger evidence to inform policymaking.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: ITI therefore encourages the Commission to take a thorough and evidence-based policy approach, ensuring that the forthcoming impact assessment carefully evaluates the necessity and added value of any future Digital Fairness Act initiative in light of the existing digital framework. Any new intervention must be subject to a rigorous evaluation to avoid overlapping obligations and legal uncertainty for consumers, businesses, and regulators that could potentially undermine the EU's broader Simplification Agenda.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: III. Conduct a Thorough Mapping of Existing Legislation

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: The current EU regulatory landscape already addresses many of the practices cited in the Call for Evidence. Before introducing new regulatory requirements under a future Digital Fairness Act, the Commission should carry out a comprehensive mapping exercise of the existing digital and consumer protection rules applicable to online services and platforms. This exercise would be beneficial and complement the prior Fitness Check as the interaction between these instruments is complex, and implementation across Member States remains uneven. Without such a mapping, potential new DFA requirements would risk unnecessarily duplicating obligations already found in existing instruments, creating overlaps or contradictions, and increasing legal uncertainty and compliance burdens for businesses.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: The DSA, DMA, AI Act, GDPR, ePrivacy Directive, AVMSD, Political Advertising Regulation, CRD, UCPD, and other relevant legislation together form a dense and evolving framework. These instruments were designed to address key challenges in the digital environment, including consumer protection, platform accountability, algorithmic design, advertising practices and user consent. The Modernisation Directive already introduced several measures now being contemplated for a potential DFA, such as extending the CRD to free digital services for which consumers do not pay money but provide personal data, requiring transparency

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: about personalized pricing, the UCPD Annex I "Blacklist" and creating new transparency requirements about consumer reviews and the ranking of search results. In 2021, the Commission issued guidance to clarify these amendments.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: A thorough mapping would allow the Commission to properly identify where overlaps, inconsistencies, or implementation/oversight gaps exist and whether these can be resolved through existing tools. It would also support a more informed impact assessment, ensuring that any potential new measures are proportionate and targeted. In our view, such a mapping is a prerequisite for any decision regarding new legislation under the DFA. It is also consistent with the Commission's broader efforts to streamline and simplify the EU's digital rulebook.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
II. Adhering to Better Regulation Principles and Conducting an Evidence-Based Impact Assessment
Allowing time to implement and assess existing legislation
paragraphs.text: Below, ITI outlines examples of how existing instruments already address the concerns raised in the Call for Evidence.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Harmful online choice architecture (Dark patterns)
paragraphs.text: Consumer choice is crucial for digital fairness and competition in the EU. Our members support consumers in making informed decisions and provide meaningful control over their digital experience which is an essential part of the customer experience.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Harmful online choice architecture (Dark patterns)
paragraphs.text: At the same time, the EU has taken significant steps in this area. Several existing legislative instruments already address deceptive design practices commonly referred to as dark patterns. The UCPD, including its Annex, includes banned practices and prohibits "misleading actions and omissions" (Article 6 and 7) and aggressive practices that significantly impair consumers' freedom of choice through undue influence. The DSA also prohibits designing interfaces that mislead users or manipulate their choices via so-called 'dark patterns' on online platforms. For instance, Article 25 (1) prohibits providers from designing, organising or operating "their online interfaces in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients of their service or in a way that otherwise materially distorts or impairs the ability of the recipients of their service to make free and informed decisions". Article 34 also requires platforms to mitigate systemic risks associated with deceptive interfaces. The recently published guidelines on the protection of minors pursuant to Article 28 of the DSA also include key recommendations for platforms to ensure that minors' lack of commercial literacy is not exploited and that they are not exposed to practices that may be manipulative, such as those that could lead to unintended spending.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Harmful online choice architecture (Dark patterns)
paragraphs.text: More broadly the GDPR sets clear rules on when and how informed consent must be obtained and withdrawn, helping prevent manipulative consent practices. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has also issued guidelines specifically addressing deceptive design patterns in social media interfaces, providing concrete recommendations to ensure that platform designs do not infringe the GDPR. Building on this, the EDPB adopted new guidelines regarding the interplay between the GDPR and the DSA, which provide further clarity on how the two regulations jointly tackle these practices.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Harmful online choice architecture (Dark patterns)
paragraphs.text: Article 5 of the AI Act includes provisions prohibiting the use of subliminal techniques or "purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques" that impair user autonomy, where such techniques are likely to cause significant harm. For example, recent Commission guidelines

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Harmful online choice architecture (Dark patterns)
paragraphs.text: on these provisions explicitly reference practices such as ‘design features that aim to influence consumers’ purchasing decisions’ without their conscious awareness and causing significant harm. On top of that, the guidelines recognize that ‘dark patterns’ should be considered as manipulative or deceptive techniques under the AI Act, to the extent that they cause significant harm. Finally, the guidelines also recognize the relevance of the AI Act’s provisions to advancing consumer protection in line with the UCPD and preventing consumer harm.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Harmful online choice architecture (Dark patterns)
paragraphs.text: Article 50 of the AI Act also contains transparency obligations for both AI developers and deployers, requiring - among others - to inform natural persons when they are interacting with an AI system and to label certain AI-generated content such as deepfakes and informational text. Such requirements are aimed at increasing fairness, transparency and trust in the online ecosystem, including as it pertains to user interfaces, and supports users’ overall online wellbeing.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: Issues relating to online consent and personalization are already thoroughly addressed within the legal logic and long-standing principles of the GDPR and the e-Privacy Directive. These matters fall squarely within the scope of privacy laws and should not be reframed or reinterpreted through a consumer protection lens, such as under a potential DFA that would add an extra layer of confusion. Maintaining uniformity and consistency with the GDPR’s established framework is essential to ensure legal certainty, coherence and avoid fragmentation. Any proven consumer issues related to digital advertising and personalization should be carefully assessed under the existing EU regulatory framework. Should a potential DFA address these practices, it should clearly set out how it interacts with existing laws, including the GDPR, the ePrivacy Directive, the DMA, the DSA and the AI Act. Above all, it must avoid duplicating or reinterpreting provisions already regulated under these frameworks, as such overlaps risk inconsistent enforcement and eroding legal certainty for both data subjects and data controllers.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: The GDPR provides a comprehensive framework for the lawful processing of personal data, including requirements for processing sensitive data (Article 9) and data profiling (Articles 4(4), 21, and 22). It requires organizations to obtain valid consent for certain types of data-driven targeting, ensure transparency, and uphold individuals’ rights, including the right to object and the right not to be subject to automated decision-making where it produces legal or similarly significant effects. Most notably, GDPR considers processing personal data to evaluate an individual’s personal aspects, such as their economic situation, health, preferences, or interests, as well as processing vulnerable individuals, to be "high-risk" activities necessitating a data protection impact assessment (DPIA) (see GDPR Recital 75 and Article 35). The GDPR already requires controllers to identify and implement measures to address the risks revealed via DPIAs.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: itic.org

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: The ePrivacy Directive requires prior consent for any access to or storage of information on a user's device, covering technologies like cookies or similar technologies typically used for personalised ads and content.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: The AI Act's prohibitions on harmful AI systems already apply to AI techniques that facilitate unlawful personalization. For example, Article 5(1)(b) of the Act prohibits AI systems that exploit an individual's vulnerabilities—such as those related to age, physical or mental disability, or socioeconomic status—with the intent of materially distorting their behavior in a way that causes significant harm. Similarly, the Commission's guidelines on prohibited practices also note that the prohibition in Article 5(1)(a) on harmful subliminal techniques should cover "personalized manipulation" that creates significant harm.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: Under the DSA, online platforms are already required to assess and adequately mitigate risks stemming from their recommender systems, including risks for the mental health of users and the dissemination of harmful content arising from the engagement-based design of these algorithms. In accordance with Article 26 of the DSA, platforms must clearly identify personalized ads, disclose advertiser identities and targeting parameters. Moreover, Article 27 of the DSA requires providers of online platforms that use recommender systems to explain in their terms and conditions, "in plain and intelligible language, the main parameters used in their recommender systems, as well as any options for the recipients of the service to modify or influence those main parameters". The DSA also enables user control and choice when it comes to recommender systems. It asks platforms to "make available a functionality that allows the recipient of the service to select and to modify at any time their preferred option" – and ensure that functionality is "directly and easily accessible from the specific section of the online platform's online interface where the information is being prioritised". Additionally, the DSA introduces further safeguards for vulnerable groups, including minors. For instance, Article 28 prohibits targeted ads on platforms based on profiling minors or using sensitive personal data. The recently published guidelines on the protection of minors pursuant to Article 28 of the DSA mention that platforms should ensure their recommender systems do not rely on behavioral data processing so extensive that it captures all or most of a minor's activity, as this could make minors feel their private life is being constantly monitored. In addition, Articles 38 and 39 of the DSA, impose additional obligations on very large online platforms ("VLOPs") and very large online search engines ("VLOSEs"), including additional transparency requirements.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: The DMA places additional obligations on gatekeepers. Under Article 5(2) of the DMA, gatekeepers are prohibited from combining or cross-using personal data from different services without the user's explicit consent.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: Finally, legal texts touching on aspects of advertising also exist in the UCPD, CRD, Regulation on the transparency and targeting of political advertising, and Platform to Business Regulation. For instance, the UCPD prohibits traders from exploiting consumer vulnerabilities or distorting consumer decision-making and applies to both general and targeted marketing.

section_path: ITI Response to the European Commission’s Call for Evidence on the Digital Fairness Act Proposal
Personalization
paragraphs.text: A further dimension is introduced by the revised Product Liability Directive (PLD, Directive (EU) 2024/2853). Article 6 expands recoverable damage to include medically recognized harm to psychological health. Accordingly, harms attributable to AI-enabled personalization or recommender systems that result from a defective product and cause psychological injury may fall within the PLD's compensatory regime.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: Industry actors are actively deploying tools that encourage healthy usage habits. This includes parental control tools that allow parents to manage children's digital experiences, for example by setting screen time limits and disabling specific features. In addition, many services offer general screen time management tools for users. These solutions allow for differentiated access to online experiences through consumer controls to allow minors (or responsible adults) to set boundaries for and modulate these experiences based on the product or service, the trader providing it, or even based on their current mindset. Concerns around overuse of digital services and so-called addictive design are also addressed under existing legislation.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: For instance, the DSA addresses this as part of systemic risk assessments and also via provisions on profile-based recommender systems (Articles 28, 34, and 38). This includes an obligation for VLOPs and VLOSEs to assess and mitigate risks to the rights of children, which may include exposure to content that affects mental health or the use of interfaces likely to induce addictive behaviour. The DSA also requires platforms to offer alternative recommender systems not based on profiling.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: The recently published guidelines on the protection of minors pursuant to Article 28 of the DSA also include recommendations on disabling by default features that contribute to excessive use. Given that the guidelines were published on 14 July 2025, only three days before the launch of the DFA Call for Evidence, it is difficult to assess the state of minor's safety online without first allowing companies time to digest and implement these obligations. The guidelines address, among other areas:

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: Interface design (Section 6.4, paras. 60–61): Platforms must ensure minors are not exposed to persuasive design features aimed predominantly at engagement that may lead to extensive use, such as infinite scroll, video autoplay, and virtual rewards.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: Commercial practices (Section 6.6, paras. 68–69): Platforms must ensure minors are not exposed to manipulative techniques, such as scarcity cues, intermittent or random rewards, or persuasive design techniques, that may drive impulsive or unwanted spending or addictive behaviours.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: These detailed requirements, combined with the DSA's risk assessment obligations, already oblige platforms to address risks linked to addictive design and to adapt accordingly. Relatedly, age assurance is a critical component of a comprehensive online safety strategy, but it must be implemented with nuance and precision to be effective. A risk-based approach is the most appropriate way forward, aligning with frameworks like the DSA's Article 28 guidelines. This ensures that children receive appropriate protection without needlessly restricting their access to valuable online experiences. Mandating blunt measures like age verification for all adult users across the entire internet is an overly broad approach that creates unnecessary friction and privacy trade-offs, particularly where no significant risk is present.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: The UCPD prohibits practices that exploit known consumer vulnerabilities to unduly influence decision-making, and certain design techniques may already fall under its scope. The Commission's guidance accompanying the UCPD includes examples of addictive design. In addition, Article 5(1) (b) of the AI Act also addresses concerns related to addictive design by

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: prohibiting AI systems that exploit users' vulnerabilities where such practices are likely to cause significant harm. The prohibition of harmful subliminal or manipulative techniques in Article 5(1)(a) of the AI Act is also relevant. For example, the Commission's own guidelines on the AI Act's prohibited practices note that the prohibition in Article 5(1)(b) covers "addiction-like practices" or "deliberate addictive and exploitative design features" that could seriously harm people, for example by exploiting vulnerabilities related to age (e.g., children) and that the prohibition in Article 5(1)(a) covers techniques related to the 'addictive design of AI systems or AI-enabled applications'³ that cause significant harm.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
paragraphs.text: When addressing such practices, we encourage the European Commission to adopt clear, outcome-focused principles rather than prescriptive rules or technology-specific prohibitions, such as outright bans on particular design features or functions. The Article 28 Guidelines are a good example of this approach, and we believe it would be wise to first let these guidelines take root. Finally, the European Commission is already planning an EU-wide inquiry into digital wellbeing. It will be important for the European Commission to work with a wide range of stakeholders, including providers of relevant online services, academics, teachers, and parents, and to approach this from a broad perspective, also taking into account relevant economic and societal factors. Existing industry-led best practices should be considered by policymakers, which have often proven effective at tackling risks in this context.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: Subscription services not only increase access to cutting-edge technology by lowering costs but they also enable companies to quickly deliver new product features to their customers and enhanced and real-time security that protects consumers against the latest exploits. Furthermore, given the cost to operate AI, subscription services already are and will continue to play a role in facilitating the ongoing development of the emerging AI startup and business economy. Subscriptions are essential to ensuring customers have access to the latest AI innovations at the pace at which they are being developed.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: The Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the UCPD and the DSA already enable smooth cancellation of subscription contracts with the focus on easy withdrawals and not making unsubscribing unnecessarily difficult.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: For example, as of 2023, the insertion of Article 11a into the CRD via Directive (EU) 2023/2673 requires that, for distance contracts concluded through an online interface, traders must provide a prominent and easily accessible withdrawal function which shall be labelled with the words "withdraw from contract here" or an unambiguous corresponding formulation in an easily legible way. The UCPD in Article 9(d) identifies obstacles to contract termination as an aggressive commercial practice, offering a solid legal foundation to tackle so-called subscription traps. This is also tackled in the Commission's 2021 guidance on the UCPD that states that unsubscribing should be as easy as subscribing. In addition, both the CRD and UCPD already require that clear information be provided on pricing, payment obligations prior to contract conclusion, as well as on the duration, renewal, and termination of subscription contracts. Issues relating to the conversion of free trials into paid subscriptions are also already addressed under these frameworks, which provide rules on pricing transparency,

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: ITI Promoting Innovation Worldwide itic.org

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: renewal, and cancellation. The most effective path forward is therefore not to introduce new prescriptive requirements, but to strengthen oversight to ensure consistent application of these protections to free-trial and auto-renewal flows. In this context, any future policy measures should remain technology-neutral and principle-based. It is also important to recognize that users already complete a full purchase flow when signing up for free trials and collecting payment details at this stage helps verify age eligibility and prevent fraud.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Subscriptions
paragraphs.text: Finally, the DSA in Article 25 prohibits platforms from designing their interfaces in ways that can impair the ability of users to make free and informed decisions and thereby make the procedure for terminating a service more difficult than subscribing to it. The Commission is also empowered under the DSA to issue additional guidelines to address such practices should consumers encounter issues.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Influencer marketing
paragraphs.text: The UCPD, Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD) and DSA already include provisions covering the disclosure of commercial communications, bans on hidden advertising and transparency requirements, including on the labelling of ads and endorsements. The European Commission’s **Influencer Legal Hub** resource provides further clarity on applicable rules.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Influencer marketing
paragraphs.text: According to Article 26(2) of the DSA, providers of online platforms must provide consumers with a functionality to declare whether the content they provide is or contains commercial communications. The provider of online platforms must also ensure that other recipients of the service can identify in a clear and unambiguous manner and in real time, including through prominent markings, that the content is or contains commercial communications. The aforementioned DSA provision complements Article 28b(2) of the AVMSD applying to video content, which requires online service providers to inform users clearly when programs or user-generated videos contain commercial communication and to provide the means for the users who upload user-generated videos to declare whether such videos contain audiovisual commercial communications as far as they know or can be reasonably expected to know. The updated guidance of 17 December 2021 on the implementation of the UCPD, the CRD and the Price Indication Directive also provided legal interpretation on obligations of online platforms and “influencers” with regard to “influencer marketing” and prohibits practices that do not make clear that a trader has paid for the promotion of a product in editorial content, and that the disclosures must “be sufficiently salient to adequately inform the average or vulnerable consumer that receives the content”. It also cannot require consumers to take additional steps, such as by clicking on a link to understand if the content is paid promotion.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Influencer marketing
paragraphs.text: In addition to the regulatory framework, the European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA) and national advertising Self-regulatory Organisations (SROs) equip influencers and content creators across Europe with the knowledge and tools to uphold high standards of advertising transparency and responsibility. EASA’s AdEthics programme combines training on responsible advertising practices, and a certification, which supports responsible communication through ongoing monitoring of the creators’ published content. EASA has also drawn up the database DiscloseMe where influencers can easily access transparency disclosures for each country, language and social media platform. We believe EU-level recognition of these programs and tools would support their success.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Influencer marketing
paragraphs.text: While discussions on influencer marketing now also extend to potential restrictions on the types of content influencers can post, or placing greater responsibility on brands for influencer content, blanket bans or one-size-fits-all prohibitions are not the right approach. A principles-based approach, building on the existing legal framework and industry best practices, would be more effective. Rather than imposing new obligations on actors that are already compliant, we would encourage efforts to educate consumers, empowering them to make informed, safe, and self-determined choices when engaging with digital content.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
IV. Streamlining Implementation and Oversight
paragraphs.text: As recognized in the Digital Fairness Fitness Check, the EU consumer protection acquis has only partially achieved its objectives, primarily due to challenges in implementation and oversight, for example in cross-border inquiries and enforcement cases. These issues stem not from a lack of legislation, but from the practical challenges of ensuring consistent, effective implementation across Member States and guidance for companies on how to comply. It is therefore essential to focus efforts on improving how existing rules are implemented in practice and how regulators monitor and oversee this. The introduction of new legislation, such as a potential DFA, would not resolve these structural implementation and oversight challenges. Rather, the Commission should prioritize strengthening coordination and oversight of existing rules and addressing the root causes of implementation and compliance gaps.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Improving implementation of existing rules
paragraphs.text: A first priority should be to make oversight more effective and predictable. Authorities should adopt a risk-based approach that allows them to concentrate on the most harmful and systemic infringements. At the same time, guidance should be revised and clarified to provide companies with practical tools to comply and legal certainty on their obligations. Any new potential initiative should complement the existing framework, be grounded in user-centric principles, and focus on tangible outcomes for consumers, including by encouraging informal resolution processes between companies and consumers. The promotion and adoption of relevant technical standards can further support consistent compliance across the EU.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Strengthening oversight structure and capacity
paragraphs.text: A key factor contributing to the challenges identified is that the expansion of the EU's digital regulatory landscape has increased the number of authorities responsible for overlapping areas of legislation, often operating with narrowly defined mandates and limited cooperation mechanisms. This fragmented structure increases the risk of inconsistent interpretation, overlapping obligations, and enforcement gaps. In addition, EU laws such as the DSA, the DMA, the Data Act, generally enter into effect and become enforceable before the necessary oversight structures and tools are in place. For example, there may be delays in Member State legislators transposing EU rules into national law or under-resourced enforcement bodies. EU rules can also become legally binding before the relevant regulator (a Member State regulator, EU-level body or the Commission) has drafted and consulted on actionable and workable guidance that supports company compliance.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Strengthening oversight structure and capacity
paragraphs.text: Member States should therefore enhance the capacity of their national enforcement authorities, including by providing sufficient staffing, expertise, and financial resources to apply and oversee the consumer protection framework in the digital age. In addition, improved coordination across regulatory bodies at the national and European level is essential. For instance, existing mechanisms and authorities such as the national Digital Service Coordinators under the DSA should be more effectively leveraged. Member State authorities should be encouraged to collaborate on areas of shared responsibility following national models like France's National coordination network for digital services regulation. In addition, EU-level bodies should consider forming structured or ad hoc fora to facilitate alignment across frameworks such as the GDPR, DSA, DMA, AI Act, and other relevant frameworks. The Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Network has proven its ability to quickly and efficiently tackle cross-border violations, highlighting the power of coordinated consumer protection efforts respecting the country-of-origin principle.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Promoting EU-Level harmonization and joint guidance
paragraphs.text: Finally, the development of joint guidance from relevant authorities in key areas harmonized at EU level is critical to ensure that companies can implement legal requirements effectively where frameworks intersect. Guidance should clarify the interaction between different frameworks, provide practical compliance expectations, and help prevent divergent national approaches. Such guidance should be subject to engagement and public consultation, allowing stakeholders to contribute insights and flag implementation challenges.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
V. Safeguarding Legal Certainty and Balanced Consumer Protection in the Digital Age
Fairness by Design is an established principle of EU consumer law
paragraphs.text: Fairness by Design is the foundation of the online services provided by ITI's members. It is indeed key to earning consumer trust, which in turn affects whether people choose a service and are willing to share data or pay for it.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
V. Safeguarding Legal Certainty and Balanced Consumer Protection in the Digital Age
Fairness by Design is an established principle of EU consumer law
paragraphs.text: However, a new explicit Fairness by Design obligation is unnecessary and would add little to consumer protection. Existing consumer law already embeds the principle from the outset. For example, it is reflected in:

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
V. Safeguarding Legal Certainty and Balanced Consumer Protection in the Digital Age
Fairness by Design is an established principle of EU consumer law
paragraphs.text: Pre- and post-contractual information obligations and prohibitions on dark patterns; and

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
V. Safeguarding Legal Certainty and Balanced Consumer Protection in the Digital Age
Fairness by Design is an established principle of EU consumer law
paragraphs.text: General rules expressed in the negative, notably Article 5(1) of the UCPD: "Unfair commercial practices shall be prohibited".

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
V. Safeguarding Legal Certainty and Balanced Consumer Protection in the Digital Age
Fairness by Design is an established principle of EU consumer law
paragraphs.text: The GDPR also enshrines the principle of fairness under Article 5(1) and requires any processing to be fair and lawful. Article 13 further specifies that individuals must be transparently informed when personal data concerning them are collected.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
V. Safeguarding Legal Certainty and Balanced Consumer Protection in the Digital Age
Fairness by Design is an established principle of EU consumer law
paragraphs.text: A broad, positively worded catch-all duty to secure a fair online environment would be redundant and risk legal uncertainty. The same outcomes can be achieved by clarifying and enforcing existing rules through case law or guidance. Where specific problems are identified, they should be addressed with targeted, issue-specific legislation rather than an ambiguous Fairness by Design obligation.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: The burden of proof standard should remain. Reversing it to presume non-compliance by traders, including small and medium-sized businesses, offers limited added value and risks disproportionate consequences. Establishing new requirements for businesses to demonstrate compliance with consumer laws would introduce ex-ante rules, lowering the threshold for such an approach below other sectors (e.g., financial services, telecoms). Such an approach would be disproportionate and unjustified, introducing unreasonable additional efforts and costs that could hinder competitiveness and ultimately leave consumers with higher prices and fewer options. The revised PLD already provides consumers with effective resources by easing the burden of proof in complex cases. Article 10 establishes rebuttable presumptions of defectiveness and causation, while maintaining a balanced mechanism that allows manufacturers to defend themselves. This ensures consumer protection without shifting the default burden of proof across the board.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: It must also be taken into account that the vast majority of consumer laws already require disclosure of mandatory information to consumers as well as other information that must be transparent throughout the consumer journey, and hence readily available to consumers.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: The concept of the "average consumer who is reasonably well informed and reasonably observant and circumspect" has more than two decades of consistent interpretation by the European Court of Justice and has proven to be an effective and flexible benchmark. By also taking into account social, cultural, and linguistic factors, it provides a technology-neutral standard that supports a high level of consumer protection while maintaining legal certainty and promoting both free trade and business competitiveness in the Union.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Where appropriate, the notion of the "average member of the group" provides an additional layer of protection when a trader foreseeably targets a specific group of consumers and can reasonably be expected to consider their particular characteristics. This provides further safeguards when a commercial practice is directed at a group of consumers who, because of their common characteristics, do not fit in the "average consumer" standard.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Additionally, the existing concept of "vulnerable consumers", for example, those whose ability to make informed decisions may be affected by mental or physical infirmity, age, or credulity, introduces a higher standard of protection based on individual circumstances that are objectively verifiable and foreseeable. This standard remains reasonable for traders, who can adapt their commercial practices to take into account vulnerabilities that are common to a specific group of consumers. While the need to protect vulnerable users is paramount, a potential DFA should avoid overly broad definitions of "vulnerability" (e.g., "emotional distress"). Vague criteria such as "negative mental states" risk creating legal uncertainty and

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: making compliance impractical, particularly for complex systems that require automation like advertising, which processes thousands of ads per millisecond. Increased uncertainty may lead to regulatory complexity, directly impacting the businesses that rely on those advertisements to thrive, and small and medium businesses in particular. Instead, any potential DFA should rely on the existing, flexible EU consumer law framework. The "average consumer" standard, and its well-established segment for 'vulnerable consumers,' is a robust and sufficient benchmark to capture harmful situations online in a flexible manner without jeopardizing legal certainty.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Amending or supplementing these well-established concepts with additional benchmarks or factors would undermine legal certainty to the detriment of traders. Businesses offering products or services to the general public cannot reasonably be expected to account for the individual characteristics and personal circumstances that shape each consumer's purchasing decision. Such new benchmarks or factors would also require the additional collection of personal data by traders, with resulting risks to privacy as well as - in some cases - conflict with the GDPR and DSA rules preventing online platforms from collecting additional personal data, for instance on minors. The needs of specific vulnerable groups are best addressed under existing frameworks and practices, for example to safeguard children, people with disabilities and the elderly. Consumers can identify themselves in these categories and benefit from the adaptations designed to help them while maintaining general standards for other consumers.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: The objective to achieve a higher level of protection of consumers by taking into account their individual characteristics and personal circumstances can rather be achieved through other means, for example consumer information and education campaigns.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Addressing misuse of the right of withdrawal in a balanced manner

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: The CRD gives consumers a 14-day withdrawal period for online purchases, intended for consumers to inspect a product. However, there are growing cases of misuse and even fraud, such as returns of used or damaged goods, non-original or incomplete products, or even substitutions with entirely different items. These practices create significant costs not only for businesses but also for the environment, as many returned goods must be discarded, leading to unnecessary waste. Targeted safeguards could help address this issue, while continuing to uphold strong consumer rights.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: VI. Ensuring Simplification within the Digital Omnibus Context

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Simplification must be the guiding principle for the European Commission's work when assessing the necessity of the DFA or any other planned regulation. As outlined above, the policy options presented in the Call for Evidence are already addressed by a recent set of laws that remain in the early stages of implementation. Should the Commission's impact assessment identify clear evidence of market failure and consumer harm, thereby justifying new legislation to fill a gap in the law, it should be used as an opportunity to strengthen harmonized application and oversight to address these harms. Given the ongoing

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: simplification efforts under the Digital Package (digital omnibus), any potential DFA initiative should take a holistic view of both processes. Simplification or streamlining of the existing legislative acquis applicable to platforms should be pursued through the ongoing omnibus efforts, rather than through new standalone measures under the DFA.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: To achieve simplification, the Commission should adopt a three-pronged strategy to guide its approach to digital fairness:

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Integrate Simplification from the Start. For simplification to be effective, it must be a foundational principle of good regulation, not a mere add-on. Simplification should be at the center of the discussions on a potential DFA from the very beginning. The Commission should ensure its legislative initiatives are cohesive, specifically by aligning the work on a potential DFA with the upcoming Digital Omnibus. This prevents potential new regulations from duplicating or conflicting with existing ones, ensuring future laws are clear and impactful.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Implement a Holistic Strategy. Simplification efforts should be comprehensive and not restricted to a single sector of the economy, even though supporting small and medium-sized enterprises is crucial. The digital landscape is an interconnected ecosystem, and friction at any point within the value chain negatively impacts all actors, from startups to large corporations. The goal should be to establish a unified, trust-based framework that guarantees citizens' rights are consistently protected across the Union, while providing businesses with clear, predictable, and dependable rules.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Embrace a Risk-Based Approach. A risk-based approach should guide regulatory simplification to ensure digital fairness for all consumers. Rather than applying uniform, one-size-fits-all model, rules should focus on the most significant and high-impact risks. Such a shift can free up companies to concentrate on developing innovative and user-friendly products. This shift can reduce unnecessary compliance pressures, allowing companies to invest in innovation and user-focused solutions, and encourage an industry culture that actively advances online experience while delivering safer and more reliable services.

section_path: Concerns with overuse of digital services and content (so-called addictive design)
Upholding established consumer protection standards
paragraphs.text: Finally, we welcome that the Call for Evidence considers whether certain information could be provided to consumers exclusively in digital form. This would allow users to access more timely and relevant information, available in multiple languages, while reducing environmental waste.
